content.text: A conversation flow wireframe is a low-fidelity diagram that maps every turn of an AI-driven dialogue — what the system says, what slots it needs to fill, what branches exist for success, failure, and correction, and where escalation occurs. Script prototypes complement wireframes by expressing the same logic as executable turn sequences: a numbered exchange that a developer can run against a chatbot before any production code exists. Together they form the lo-fi prototype artifact that aligns product, engineering, and safety reviewers on conversational behavior.

attrs.alt: Conversation Flow Wireframe — Travel Assistant Booking Flow

content.text: What Wireframes Capture That PRDs Cannot

A PRD section on conversational behavior specifies goals and constraints: what the assistant must and must not do. A wireframe specifies mechanics: in which order slots are collected, how many retries are allowed before fallback, whether a correction loops back to an earlier node, and which intent triggers an escalation. These details are too granular for a PRD but too critical to leave to engineering judgment.
: determines which information the user must provide before the assistant can act — changing the order changes perceived friction
: defines what the system says when a slot cannot be filled — this is product copy, not engineering logic
: shows which nodes the user can revisit after confirming — prevents the assistant from getting stuck in a confirmed state
: specifies the exact condition (retry count, out-of-scope intent, policy refusal) that switches from AI to human or graceful exit
: captures intent-routing decisions that are non-obvious from a requirements list alone
Wireframes are the cheapest place to discover that a conversational flow is impossible to complete without the user knowing a piece of information they cannot reasonably be expected to have.
Anatomy of a Conversation Flow Wireframe
A standard GenAI wireframe uses six node types and three edge types. The exact visual style (boxes, ovals, colors) does not matter as long as reviewers can distinguish them consistently.
First AI turn — sets context and expectation
AI requests a specific piece of information from the user
Slot name, required/optional, retry limit, reprompt text
System performs an operation (search, lookup, confirm)
Action type, latency expectation, displayed output
AI presents summary and awaits user approval
Flow cannot proceed — AI offers alternative path
Slot filled correctly, intent matched, user confirms
Slot not filled but retry budget remains
Retry exhausted, out-of-scope intent, policy block
User says CHANGE or UNDO after a confirmation node

A script prototype is a numbered turn sequence expressed in plain text or code. Each turn specifies: who speaks (USER or AI), the content, the slot being filled (if any), and the system state after the turn. Script prototypes are the wireframe made executable — they can be checked manually, used as chatbot test cases, or imported into an evaluation harness.

# SCRIPT: travel-booking-happy-path
# Flow: Booking → Destination → Dates → Confirmation → Book
# Persona: urban_professional (AI-literate, goal-oriented)

TURN 1
  SYSTEM: session_start
  AI: "Where would you like to go, and when?"
  SLOT_STATE: { destination: null, depart_date: null, return_date: null }

TURN 2
  USER: "I want to fly to Tokyo next month"
  AI: "Got it — Tokyo. What dates work for you?"
  SLOT_STATE: { destination: "Tokyo", depart_date: null, return_date: null }
  SLOTS_FILLED: [destination]
  BRANCH: CONTINUE → A3

TURN 3
  USER: "August 10th to August 20th"
  AI: "Perfect. I found flights from £620 return. Shall I book?"
  SLOT_STATE: { destination: "Tokyo", depart_date: "2025-08-10", return_date: "2025-08-20" }
  SLOTS_FILLED: [depart_date, return_date]
  BRANCH: CONTINUE → A4 (all slots filled)

TURN 4
  USER: "Yes"
  AI: "Your Tokyo trip is booked. Confirmation sent to your email."
  INTENT_MATCHED: CONFIRM_YES
  ACTION: BOOK_FLIGHT(destination="Tokyo", depart="2025-08-10", return="2025-08-20")
  BRANCH: TERMINAL → BOOKED
Required Script Variants per Wireframe Node Graph
A single wireframe represents dozens of possible paths. The minimum set of script prototypes that must be authored for each flow before engineering begins:
All required slots filled on first attempt; user confirms
One slot requires a reprompt before being filled
User says CHANGE at confirmation, corrects one slot
loop_back edge traversed; updated slot value in final state
User asks something outside the flow mid-conversation
Refusal or redirect issued; slot state unchanged
Low AI-literacy user gives ambiguous or emotional input
Reprompt is plain-language; no jargon; HITL available
Every flow must have at minimum a happy path and a fallback script. Correction loop and out-of-scope scripts are required for any flow with a confirmation node or policy-gated slots.

Once a wireframe is reviewed and approved, engineering maps each node to a handler function or a dialogue manager state. The script prototypes become the test cases for those handlers. The mapping is explicit and documented in the technical spec appended to the PRD.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable


@dataclass
class WireframeNode:
    node_id: str
    node_type: str              # slot_capture | action | confirmation | fallback | terminal
    handler_function: str       # Python function name in dialogue manager
    test_script_ids: list[str]  # ConversationScript ids that cover this node

    def coverage_complete(self, authored_scripts: set[str]) -> bool:
        return all(sid in authored_scripts for sid in self.test_script_ids)


# Example mapping table produced during design handoff
NODE_MAP: list[WireframeNode] = [
    WireframeNode(
        node_id="A1",
        node_type="greeting",
        handler_function="handle_greeting",
        test_script_ids=["travel-happy-path", "travel-out-of-scope"],
    ),
    WireframeNode(
        node_id="A2",
        node_type="slot_capture",
        handler_function="capture_destination",
        test_script_ids=["travel-happy-path", "travel-retry-destination", "travel-fallback"],
    ),
    WireframeNode(
        node_id="A3",
        node_type="slot_capture",
        handler_function="capture_dates",
        test_script_ids=["travel-happy-path", "travel-date-validation-error"],
    ),
    WireframeNode(
        node_id="A4",
        node_type="confirmation",
        handler_function="confirm_booking",
        test_script_ids=["travel-happy-path", "travel-correction-loop"],
    ),
    WireframeNode(
        node_id="F1",
        node_type="fallback",
        handler_function="handle_fallback",
        test_script_ids=["travel-fallback"],
    ),
]


def design_coverage_report(authored_scripts: set[str]) -> dict:
    complete = [n for n in NODE_MAP if n.coverage_complete(authored_scripts)]
    missing = [n for n in NODE_MAP if not n.coverage_complete(authored_scripts)]
    return {
        "nodes_covered": len(complete),
        "nodes_missing_scripts": [n.node_id for n in missing],
        "ready_for_engineering": len(missing) == 0,
    }
No retry edges, no fallback node
Every slot-capture node must have at least a retry edge and a retry-exhausted → fallback edge
Retry count unspecified or set to 'until filled'
Cap retry at 2–3; after cap, branch to fallback — never loop forever
Confirmation node has no CHANGE edge
Add a correction edge back to the earliest affected slot-capture node
Date range validity assumed but not modelled
Add a validation edge from date slot with 'invalid range' → reprompt text
Only the happy path is prototyped
Author all required variants (retry, fallback, correction, out-of-scope, vulnerable)
Fallback node has ESCALATE action but no closure turn
Add AI turn confirming escalation and closing the conversation cleanly

Some slots can only be filled after another slot is known. A date-range slot is meaningless without a destination; a seat-preference slot is meaningless without a flight. Wireframes must make these dependencies explicit — not every slot-capture node is reachable from every prior node.

The slot dependency graph must be drawn on the wireframe as node ordering — nodes that depend on earlier slots are placed further down the diagram. If a dependency is conditional (skip_if), the wireframe shows two paths: one where the slot is collected and one where it is bypassed.

When a slot fails to fill, the reprompt text is a product decision that affects perceived AI quality. Vague reprompts ('I didn't understand that') are the most common conversational UX failure. Script prototypes must include the exact reprompt utterances, not a placeholder.

# SCRIPT: travel-destination-ambiguous
# Tests: how the AI handles an ambiguous destination input

TURN 1
  USER: "I want to go to Paris"
  AI: "Paris, France, or Paris, Texas?"
  SLOT_STATE: { destination: null }
  INTENT: AMBIGUOUS_SLOT_VALUE
  BRANCH: RETRY → disambiguation reprompt

TURN 2
  USER: "France"
  AI: "Paris, France — got it. What dates work for you?"
  SLOT_STATE: { destination: "CDG", destination_label: "Paris, France" }
  SLOTS_FILLED: [destination]
  BRANCH: CONTINUE → A3

# SCRIPT: travel-date-invalid-range
# Tests: AI response when return_date is before depart_date

TURN 3a
  USER: "August 20th to August 10th"
  AI: "Your return date (Aug 10) is before your departure (Aug 20).
       Could you give me the dates in order — departure first?"
  SLOT_STATE: { depart_date: null, return_date: null }
  INTENT: SLOT_VALIDATION_FAILURE (return < depart)
  BRANCH: RETRY → type-error reprompt
  RETRY_COUNT: { depart_date: 1 }

TURN 3b
  USER: "August 10th out, August 20th back"
  AI: "Perfect — Aug 10 to Aug 20. I found flights from £620."
  SLOT_STATE: { depart_date: "2025-08-10", return_date: "2025-08-20" }
  SLOTS_FILLED: [depart_date, return_date]
  BRANCH: CONTINUE → A4
Reprompt text must be specific about what was wrong and what format is needed. 'Please try again' is never acceptable reprompt copy in a reviewed script prototype.
Before a wireframe is approved for engineering handoff, it must pass a structured review. The checklist below is run by the product designer and a developer working together.
Every slot-capture node has a retry limit
Limit omitted — dialogue manager loops indefinitely
Every slot-capture node has a fallback edge when retry exhausted
No fallback defined — flow reaches a dead end
Every confirmation node has a CHANGE/UNDO edge
Correction not possible — user must restart from scratch
Slot asked before its dependency is filled
Only happy path scripted — edge cases discovered in prod
Out-of-scope intent handled at every node
AI tries to answer off-topic questions mid-flow
Distressed user gets unhelpful clarification reprompt
Escalation node has a closing AI turn
Conversation ends abruptly after ESCALATE action

Conversation flow wireframes and script prototypes are the lo-fi artifacts that translate PRD behavior requirements into reviewable, testable turn sequences. Wireframes use six node types (greeting, slot-capture, action, confirmation, fallback, terminal) and four edge types (success, retry, failure, correction) to model every path a conversation can take. Script prototypes make those paths executable as numbered turn sequences tied to specific personas and slot states.
: diagram all branches — success, retry, fallback, correction — before any code is written
: express each path as a numbered turn sequence with slot state, intent, and branch outcome at every turn
: happy path, single-slot retry, retry-exhausted fallback, correction loop, out-of-scope intent, vulnerable-user persona
: links wireframe nodes to dialogue manager functions and declares which scripts cover each node
: gates engineering handoff — no node may go into implementation without at least one covering script prototype
